forecast.rationale:
The Buckingham ward shows a clear trend toward Labour between 2021 and 2024. Labour's Nigel Jenner decisively won in 2024 with 681 votes (50.8%) versus Kevin Boram's 424 (31.7%), representing a significant 257-vote margin and substantial Conservative decline from their 2021 victory (646 votes, 49.73%). This shift reflects both local momentum and national political currents that favored Labour in 2024.

David Joseph Devoy (Labour, 135370) is the most probable winner at 0.47. Despite lacking electoral track record, he inherits Labour's strong recent performance in this ward. The party's momentum is evident across the 2021-2024 progression, and local conditions appear favorable for continued Labour success.

Kevin Boram (Conservative, 9372) remains a credible contender at 0.33 probability. He has direct prior success in this ward (2021 victor) and name recognition, but his declining vote share (from 49.73% to 31.67%) suggests weakening support. He still represents the second-strongest position.

Gabrielle Crisp (Green, 77009) receives 0.15 probability. She has demonstrated strong electoral appeal in Adur, winning St Nicolas ward in 2021 with 46.84%, and provided a substantive candidate statement. However, Green performance in Buckingham specifically was weak in 2024 (only 70 votes, 5.2%), suggesting limited local base despite Crisp's personal strength elsewhere.

William Harpley (Liberal Democrats, 111974) is given 0.01 probability. His 2024 Marine ward result (3.05%, 42 votes) indicates very limited electoral viability, and Lib Dem performance in Buckingham has consistently underperformed versus other parties.

David Stephen Cotton (Reform UK, 135369) receives 0.04 probability. Reform UK lacks any electoral history in this ward and candidate. Emerging parties typically capture 3-7% in local elections, but establish themselves gradually.

Vote share estimates reflect 2024 baseline adjusted for candidate quality variations: Labour maintains ~44% (slightly below Jenner's 50.8% to account for new candidate), Conservative holds ~30% (slight decline reflecting multi-year trend), Green increases to ~13% (Crisp as stronger candidate than 2024 rival), Reform captures ~7% (typical emerging party floor), and Lib Dem declines to ~6% (Harpley weakness).

Turnout is estimated at 42.5%, the midpoint of recent elections (2021: 42%, 2022: 41%, 2024: 44%), reflecting stable local participation patterns with no extraordinary mobilization indicators evident in the available information.

The Deddington ward shows clear Conservative dominance across five consecutive elections (2021–2025), with Conservative candidates winning every contest. Eddie Fraser Reeves, the Conservative candidate, previously won this ward in 2022 with 45.9% of the vote. The most recent by-election (May 2025, just days before this forecast) shows the Conservative candidate winning decisively at 46.9%, with a 15–20 point gap over the second-place finisher.

Annette Murphy (Labour) has demonstrated strong upward momentum in recent elections, growing from 22.4% (2022) to 27.2% (2023) to 33.2% (2024). However, her continued candidacy in 2026 carries uncertainty, and the 2025 by-election featured a different Labour candidate who received only 14.4%—suggesting some of the Labour growth may be candidate-specific to Murphy. Even if Labour continues its trajectory with Murphy, closing a 15+ percentage point gap in a single election cycle against an entrenched incumbent would be very difficult, though not impossible given national political currents. I estimate approximately 25% probability Labour wins, primarily as a tail-case reflecting extraordinary local mobilization or significant national shifts not yet fully manifest in this ward.

James Hartley (Lib Dem) has shown declining support in Deddington from 20.8% (2022) to 12.6% (2024), and did not contest the 2025 by-election. A different Lib Dem candidate (Nick Mylne) achieved 27.5% in that by-election, indicating candidate effects matter substantially. Without Hartley's recent track record, he faces an uphill contest, though Lib Dem support is unlikely to collapse entirely.

Aaron Bliss (Green) has consistently polled around 10–11% in Deddington ward elections, including 11.1% in the May 2025 by-election. This represents a stable baseline.

Gail Collingwood-Turner (Reform UK) has no prior electoral record and operates in a context where Reform has highly variable support; I estimate minimal probability given no local footprint.

Turnout is estimated at 41.5%, slightly below recent local elections (which ranged 38–44%), reflecting typical May local election patterns and no obvious mobilization catalyst.

Anthony John Payne (Reform UK, 118901) is the clear favourite as the sitting incumbent, having won the Mincinglake & Whipton by-election on 2025-05-01 with 34.83% of the vote (789 votes) and a decisive 4.5-point margin over the runner-up. Incumbents in FPTP local elections typically retain their seat, particularly when the previous election victory was recent and clear. While one year has passed since the by-election, the Reform UK candidate benefits from name recognition, campaign infrastructure, and the residual momentum of his recent victory. However, substantial uncertainty remains in a 5-candidate field.

Steve Casemore (Labour, 102891) is the principal challenger, as Labour finished second in the 2025 by-election and has historically dominated this ward (winning 2021-2024). However, Casemore presents a significantly weaker candidacy than Paula Joan Black (his predecessor in the by-election). His recent electoral record is poor: 6.11% in Bath & North East Somerset (2023) and only 2.15% in Devon County (2025: Feniton & Honiton). He has no track record in Mincinglake & Whipton itself. These factors suggest Labour's vote share will remain below their by-election performance despite the party's underlying strength in the ward.

Martin Ayres (Green, 120541) is the most consistent third-party performer, being the same candidate who achieved 11.08% (251 votes) in the 2025 by-election and 11% in the concurrent Devon County election. The Green Party has demonstrated stable ~11% support in recent local contests in this area. He is unlikely to win but could potentially place second if other candidates underperform.

David John Thompson (Conservative, 11130) enters from a different ward (Pinhoe) where he achieved 23.84% in 2024. However, the Conservative Party has experienced dramatic decline in Mincinglake & Whipton: from combined 682+513 votes (2021, two-candidate field) to just 189 votes (8.33%, 2025). Thompson's lack of local history in this specific ward and the constituency-wide Conservative weakness suggest he will underperform his Pinhoe baseline. His predecessor Baldwin got 8.33% despite local experience, so Thompson is likely to score 10-12%.

Michael Geoffrey Payne (Liberal Democrats, 11175) has run in this ward three times with consistently weak results: 5.75% (2022), 9.56% (2023), and 5.08% (2024). Despite multiple attempts, his base remains below 10%. The Richards candidate (Lib Dem) received 6.48% in the 2025 by-election. Payne's repeat candidacy offers some recognition benefit but insufficient to overcome the party's structural weakness in this ward.

Vote share predictions reflect: (1) Payne's retention of most of his by-election vote share with modest growth from absorbing independent voters and incumbency effects; (2) Labour's expected performance near the 2025 by-election despite a weaker candidate; (3) continued stable Green support; (4) Conservative underperformance from candidate and party factors; (5) persistently weak Liberal Democrat performance despite candidate experience.

Turnout is estimated at 33% based on historical ward turnout (2021-2025 range: 30-35%, average 32.8%) and the absence of major national electoral events that might drive higher participation. Local Exeter elections in this ward have shown stability in the 30-34% range.

This forecast is dominated by the 2025 by-election result, which represents the most recent and directly comparable contest in this ward. Labour (Richard Lawes, represented by Mike Sands in 2026) won by an exceptionally narrow margin of just 1 vote (689–688), with Reform UK's Nick Taylor performing remarkably strongly at 31.42%. This near-parity is the critical signal: Labour's long-standing dominance in Bowthorpe ward has been substantially eroded.

Historical context shows Labour won consistently (2021–2025), but the margin of victory has been narrowing and the competitive landscape shifting. The 2025 by-election revealed Reform UK as a major force, garnering 688 votes (31.42%), with the Greens a notable third at 430 votes (19.6%), while the Conservatives declined to 281 votes (12.8%) and Lib Dems fell to 102 votes (4.6%).

Candidate factors: Mike Sands (Labour) has prior electoral success in this ward (won in 2022 with 1170 votes), providing some incumbent advantage. Nick Taylor (Reform UK) has demonstrated strong local performance and commitment to the ward, with a detailed manifesto. Amber Smith (Green) presents a sympathetic local profile but lacks electoral track record. Rebeka Jones (Conservative) and Neil Hardman (Lib Dem) show declining party performance and weak personal records.

The race is modeled as a two-way contest between Labour and Reform UK, with Labour favored at 56% due to: (1) having won the 2025 by-election despite massive Reform pressure; (2) historical incumbent advantage; (3) traditional party strength. Reform UK's 39% probability reflects their dramatic near-miss and demonstrated mobilization capacity. Greens receive 4% chance given their consistent tertiary position but lack of breakthrough momentum. Conservative and Lib Dem receive minimal probability given continued marginalization.

Turnout is estimated at 29%, consistent with recent local election norms in Norwich (28–29% in 2022–2023), reflecting typical low engagement in local contests outside by-election spikes.

Vote shares reflect the 2025 by-election baseline with modest regression toward longer historical patterns, acknowledging that the February 2025 result was unusually competitive and may not fully persist.

Analysis of Peartree ward's electoral history reveals significant shifts in 2025 while maintaining a Lib Dem advantage. From 2021–2024, the Lib Dems dominated with vote shares between 43–52%, winning every election. Duncan Merlin Jones specifically won in 2022 with 51.8% and 770 votes, and has demonstrated strong council engagement. However, the 2025 by-election signals material change: Lib Dem support dropped to 34.2% (420 votes), while Reform UK emerged with 30.6% (375 votes)—a substantial third-place finish for a party with no prior presence in the ward. Labour declined sharply to 19.8% from its 30–40% range.

Duncan Merlin Jones remains the favorite despite this challenge: he is an incumbent councillor with verified electoral success, the Lib Dem candidate benefits from historical dominance, and they still won the 2025 by-election. However, the emergence of Reform UK as a credible challenger (gaining ~30% despite an untested candidate) substantially narrows his winning probability and reflects genuine voter volatility. The 2025 by-election results—the most recent comparable data—suggest Labour's support is fragmenting between Lib Dem (establishment) and Reform UK (protest/change vote).

Probability allocation: Lib Dem 54% reflects the historical pattern weighted against the recent challenge; Reform 38% reflects their demonstrated second-place strength in the most recent election and the anti-establishment sentiment evident in that result; Labour 5% reflects a third-place position and downward trajectory; Green and Conservative receive minimal probabilities given their historic performance at 7–14% and the absence of strong candidate profiles.

Vote share estimates weight the 2025 by-election results (most recent) more heavily than earlier elections while accounting for regression toward the longer-term trend. Turnout of 27% reflects the regular election average (26–28%) excluding the depressed by-election turnout of 22%.

The Buckingham ward has experienced a dramatic shift from Conservative to Labour control between 2021 and 2024. In 2021, Kevin Boram (Con) won decisively with 49.73% of votes. By 2024, Labour's Nigel Jenner won decisively with 681 votes (50.2%), while Boram's vote share collapsed to 31.67% (424 votes). This represents a swing of approximately 18-20 percentage points against the Conservatives.

David Joseph Devoy (Labour) benefits from Labour's recent electoral success in this ward, having won the seat just two years ago. However, uncertainty arises from Devoy being a first-time candidate with no prior electoral record, making it unknown whether he can maintain the seat with his predecessor's margin.

Kevin Boram (Conservative) represents the only candidate with direct experience in the Buckingham ward, but his declining vote share from 49.73% (2021) to 31.67% (2024) suggests significant local headwinds. His return as a candidate shows party commitment but does not reverse the demonstrated trend.

Gabrielle Crisp (Green) was elected to Adur District Council in St Nicolas ward (2021) with 46.84%, indicating some local credibility. However, historical performance in Buckingham shows the Greens are much weaker here (70 votes in 2024), and third-party challenges rarely overcome Labour's recent dominant position.

William Harpley (Lib Dem) performed extremely poorly in a nearby Marine ward in 2024 (3.05%), suggesting limited viability. David Stephen Cotton (Reform UK) has no electoral track record.

The forecast reflects Labour's momentum from the recent 2024 win while accounting for uncertainty introduced by their new candidate lacking prior electoral track record. Conservative support is unlikely to recover to 2021 levels given the demonstrated two-cycle trend, though Boram retains some local name recognition. Third parties face significant barriers in FPTP against consolidated Labour support.

Deddington ward has voted Conservative in all five elections from 2021–2025, with consistent vote shares ranging from 42–52%. Eddie Fraser Reeves (candidate_id 24118) is the Conservative candidate and previously won this seat in May 2022 with 1,416 votes (45.9%), demonstrating direct electoral strength in this ward.

Annette Murphy (Labour, candidate_id 10830) represents the strongest credible challenge. She has run in all elections since 2022 and shown a clear upward trend: 22.37% (2022) → 27.23% (2023) → 33.16% (2024). However, her performance plateaued at about 33% even as Conservative support remained stable, suggesting a structural ceiling. The 2025 by-election provides a concerning signal: a Labour candidate (Helen Margaret Oldfield, not Murphy) received only 478 votes, a dramatic collapse from Murphy's 941 in 2024, while the Conservative candidate (Zoe McLernon) surged to 1,560 votes. Though the absence of Murphy from the by-election complicates interpretation, this result indicates strong Conservative momentum and suggests any Labour gains face significant headwinds.

James Hartley (Liberal Democrat, candidate_id 77050) shows declining performance: 20.75% (2022) → 18.71% (2023) → 12.58% (2024). He did not run in the 2025 by-election. His declining trajectory makes him unlikely to win.

Aaron Bliss (Green, candidate_id 10824) has consistent but modest performance (8–11% in this ward's Cherwell elections, peaking at 11.13% in the 2025 by-election). He has never come close to winning and is not competitive.

Gail Ann Collingwood-Turner (Reform UK, candidate_id 128163) has no prior electoral record, suggesting she is unlikely to overcome the incumbent Conservative advantage.

The Conservative baseline in Deddington is strong and structural. While Labour has gained ground, the margin between Labour's peak (33.16%) and typical Conservative performance (42–48%) remains substantial. Accounting for uncertainty in national swing and Reeves' prior success in this specific seat, Conservative victory is favored at approximately 70%, with Labour as the credible but less likely challenger at 25%, and remaining candidates sharing minimal probability.

Anthony John Payne (Reform UK) is the strong favourite as the sitting incumbent councillor, having won the Mincinglake & Whipton by-election in May 2025 with 34.83% of votes (789 votes). He also demonstrated strength in the 2025 Devon County Council elections (26.8% in Barnstaple South). Incumbent advantage in single-seat FPTP elections is substantial, and his recent, direct electoral victory in this exact ward makes him the clear frontrunner.

Steve Casemore (Labour) is the main challenger. While Labour dominated this ward historically (winning in 2021, 2022, 2023, and 2024), the dramatic 2025 by-election result shows the ward has shifted. Paula Joan Black, Labour's candidate in May 2025, received 630 votes (27.95%), coming second but still 159 votes behind Payne. However, Casemore is replacing Black and has weaker recent electoral credentials (2.15% in Devon County 2025, 6.11% in Bath & North East Somerset 2023), suggesting possible weakness compared to the previous Labour candidate. This creates uncertainty but Labour still has a meaningful base in the ward.

Martin Ayres (Green Party) finished fourth in the 2025 by-election with 251 votes (11.08%) and showed consistent mid-level support (11% in Devon County 2025). However, he is unlikely to win given the clear two-horse race between Reform UK and Labour suggested by recent patterns.

Michael Geoffrey Payne (Liberal Democrats) has competed in this ward three times (2022-2024) with declining results (116→184→111 votes), suggesting weak constituency support. The Lib Dem candidate in the 2025 by-election received only 147 votes (6.52%), below his recent performance.

David John Thompson (Conservative) has no recorded history in this ward and represents a different area (Pinhoe). Conservatives have collapsed in this ward (Ian Baldwin, their 2025 by-election candidate, received only 189 votes or 8.42%), making Thompson an unlikely winner despite his 23.84% performance in neighbouring Pinhoe.

The recent by-election result, the incumbent's position, and Labour's reliance on a replacement candidate all point to Payne as the clear frontrunner, though Labour retains credible chances given their historical strength and the second-place finish in 2025.

The Bowthorpe ward has shown consistent Labour dominance from 2021–2025, with Labour winning every election with vote shares typically in the 50–55% range (approximately 1170–1201 votes in regular local elections). Mike Sands (Labour, candidate 138569) is the standing candidate who previously won this ward in 2022 with 1170 votes, providing direct electoral credibility.

However, the most recent data point—the 2025 by-election—signals significant competitive pressure. Reform UK's Nick Taylor (candidate 113142) finished extremely close to Labour's Richard Lawes (688 vs 689 votes), essentially a statistical tie. This near-victory suggests that Reform has mobilized substantial support in the ward and may have benefited from lower turnout dynamics typical of by-elections. Taylor's detailed campaign statement also demonstrates strong local commitment and knowledge.

Key considerations for the 2026 full local election:

1. **Turnout effects**: The 2025 by-election had lower turnout than regular local elections (which typically see ~28–30% in this ward). Higher turnout in 2026 could affect candidate fortunes differently. Historically, higher turnout has favored Labour in this ward.

2. **Labour's historical consistency**: Despite the 2025 scare, Labour has won four consecutive regular local elections (2021–2024) with comfortable margins of 400–900 votes. This suggests a residual structural advantage.

3. **Reform momentum**: Nick Taylor's performance represents a genuine surge in Reform support (31.42% vote share in the 2025 by-election). Reform UK has strong national momentum, but the 2025 result also shows this support narrowly fell short despite ideal by-election conditions.

4. **Other candidates**: Amber Smith (Green, 138571) represents a new candidate for the Greens, who polled 430 votes in the 2025 by-election (third place). Rebeka Jones (Conservative, 112676) saw declining Conservative support (342 votes in 2024). Neil Hardman (Liberal Democrats, 41089) has weak electoral history with very low vote shares in his prior contests (6.79% and 3.11%).

Balancing Labour's historical dominance against the unprecedented near-loss in 2025, the forecast credits Mike Sands with a modest majority, but substantially elevates Reform UK's chances compared to normal local election patterns, reflecting genuine competitive uncertainty. The three minor parties receive minimal probabilities consistent with their marginal electoral performance.

Duncan Merlin Jones (Liberal Democrat) is the clear frontrunner, but the recent 2025 by-election result indicates surprising vulnerability in this ward.

Historical context: The Lib Dems have won every single election in Peartree since 2021 (won by Russ Platt in 2021 and 2024, Duncan Merlin Jones in 2022, Simon Goldwater in 2023, and Fauzia Haider in the 2025 by-election). This represents remarkably consistent dominance.

Duncan Merlin Jones's strengths:
- Personally elected in 2022 with a strong 51.78% vote share (770 votes)
- Has served multiple terms and held senior positions (Cabinet member for Resources)
- Provides a detailed voter statement demonstrating engagement and policy vision
- Local resident with demonstrated commitment to the ward

However, the 2025 by-election (most recent actual result) shows concerning signs:
- Fauzia Haider (LD) won narrowly with 420 votes (40.5%)
- Mark Simon Biddle (Reform UK) came very close with 375 votes (36.2%)
- Reform UK's emergence as a competitive force is notable
- This margin (45 votes, ~4 percentage points) is the closest result since 2021

Reform UK's challenge:
- Ian Colpitts has no prior electoral record, which limits confidence compared to Duncan Merlin Jones
- However, Reform UK showed genuine momentum, capturing over one-third of votes in the by-election
- The by-election context (lower turnout at 22%) may have benefited insurgent movements

Labour (Darren Lewis):
- Historically consistent second place (2021: 408 votes; 2022: 497; 2023: 466; 2024: 521)
- However, Darren Lewis's only electoral record is running in Handside & Peartree in 2025 with just 13.54%, a different ward
- No strong local connection evident
- Expected to finish third

Green Party (Tanya Dickson):
- Consistent but weak performance (2024: 8.22%, 2025 by-election: 7.42%)
- Has local community credentials (forest school, community garden) but limited electoral appeal
- Appears stuck in the 90-120 vote range

Conservative (Emil Sohrab):
- Consistently fourth or fifth place historically (2021: 336; 2022: 173; 2023: 167; 2024: 175)
- No prior electoral record
- Structural weakness in this ward

Final assessment: Duncan Merlin Jones remains favored due to Lib Dem consistency and his personal record, but the 2025 by-election result suggests genuine competition from Reform UK. The narrowness of that recent result warrants material probability allocation to Colpitts, while maintaining confidence in both Jones's incumbent advantage and the ward's Lib Dem lean.

Buckingham looks competitive rather than safe. Kevin Boram has the strongest ward-specific record in the packet and has previously won here, so the Conservatives start with a real base. At the same time, the ward has recently swung away from them and the 2024 Labour winner is not standing, so that vote is not fully locked in for Labour’s new candidate. Gabrielle Crisp is a credible Green challenger because she has a strong prior local electoral record in Adur and an active community-focused profile, which could translate into a significantly better showing than the Greens had here in 2024. Reform may take a meaningful protest vote, and the Liberal Democrat candidate’s recent record is the weakest. Net: a close three-way race with the Conservatives a slight plurality favorite, but with substantial uncertainty. Turnout should land near recent Buckingham levels, around the low-40s.

Deddington looks like a Conservative-leaning ward with a persistent three-way opposition split. Eddie Fraser Reeves has strong local credentials here, including a prior Deddington win, so Conservatives remain the clear favorite. Annette Murphy is the most credible challenger: her 2024 Deddington result was strong, but it still left her behind the Conservatives, and she would likely need both a modest Conservative drop and good transfer of anti-Tory vote to win. James Hartley’s own Deddington record is respectable but generally below Labour’s recent high point, though the 2025 by-election suggests the Liberal Democrats can be competitive in the ward under favorable circumstances; I discount that somewhat because it came with a different Lib Dem candidate. Aaron Bliss is typically a steady low-double-digit Green presence. Reform is new here and looks likely to take a mid-single-digit share, probably mostly from the Conservative side, but not enough on its own to make a win likely. Turnout should be in the low 40s, a bit above 2024 on the back of a five-way contest, but probably below the 2025 by-election spike.

The ward looks like a close Reform-Labour contest, with Reform slightly ahead because Anthony John Payne is the sitting Reform winner from the 2025 by-election in the same ward. That is a strong local signal, especially versus a Labour challenger with no Exeter ward record and only weak prior results elsewhere. At the same time, Mincinglake & Whipton has historically been Labour-leaning, so Labour has a real path back if some of the 2025 fragmented anti-Labour vote re-coalesces. Conservatives and Liberal Democrats have weaker recent ward evidence, while the Green vote appears fairly stable in the low-teens but not enough to win. Turnout should be similar to the recent 30s pattern, around the mid-33s.

Bowthorpe looks like a Labour-Reform toss-up with Labour holding a narrow edge. The ward was comfortably Labour in 2021-2024, but the 2025 by-election brought Reform right up to Labour’s level, so Reform is now a credible winner rather than a long shot. I slightly favor Labour because of the ward’s longer-run Labour lean and because the Conservative/Lib Dem bases look too small to break through, but the margin is thin and forecast uncertainty is high. Green is the main third-place party and has enough local support to be competitive for third, but would need a major share shift to win. Conservatives and Liberal Democrats look further behind based on the recent ward results. Turnout should be in the low 30s, a bit above the mid/high-20s elections but not dramatically so.

Peartree looks like a Liberal Democrat-leaning ward with a long run of Lib Dem wins in the packet, and Duncan Merlin Jones is the strongest known local candidate in the field: he has prior ward victory, incumbent-style local profile, and a statement emphasizing ongoing ward work. The 2025 by-election is the main warning sign, because Reform UK ran very close to the Lib Dems there, but that was a low-turnout contest and the current Reform candidate has no local electoral record in the packet. On balance, the most likely outcome is still a Lib Dem hold, but not a lock. Reform is the clearest challenger and has a meaningful upset chance; Labour has a durable base but appears somewhat behind the top two; Conservatives and Greens look like smaller-share contenders. Turnout should likely rebound from the 22% by-election toward the high-20s, closer to recent regular-election levels.

Buckingham looks like a competitive but still major-party-led ward. The latest ward result in 2024 was a clear Labour win, which makes Labour the starting favorite, but the Labour candidate is new in the packet and the seat is open, so the edge is not overwhelming. Kevin Boram has the strongest continuity and local name recognition, having previously contested the ward and won it in earlier cycles, so Conservative remains a close second. Gabrielle Crisp is the only other candidate with meaningful elected-office experience and a public local profile, but the historical Green vote in Buckingham has usually been modest, so her win chance is real but clearly smaller. The Liberal Democrat and Reform candidates have little to no ward-specific evidence of strength, so they are long shots.

Deddington has been Conservative in every recent contest in the packet, and the Conservative baseline has been solid even when the field widened. Eddie Fraser Reeves is the only candidate here with a prior Deddington win, and the party still took 46.9% in the 2025 by-election. Labour’s Annette Murphy is the clearest challenger: her Deddington shares rose from 22.4% in 2022 to 27.2% in 2023 and 33.2% in 2024, so she has the strongest local upward trend. But she still trailed the Conservative winner by about 11 points in 2024, and the opposition is split across Labour, Liberal Democrat, Green, and Reform. James Hartley’s previous Deddington results were weaker than the Liberal Democrat’s 2025 by-election showing, which limits LD prospects relative to the Conservatives. The Greens have a stable but modest local base around 8-11%, while Reform has no ward history in the packet. Overall, Conservatives are favored, but the seat is not safe given the fragmented field and Labour’s improving past performance.

The freshest same-ward evidence is the 2025 by-election, where Reform won and Labour was the main challenger. That makes Anthony John Payne (Reform) the slight favorite, especially with incumbent advantage and local name recognition. Labour still has the strongest long-run ward history, winning comfortably in 2021-2024, so Steve Casemore remains very competitive and is the most plausible alternative winner. The absence of the 2025 independent candidate likely redistributes some votes, which helps Labour’s recovery but does not erase Reform’s recent advantage. The Green candidate has the clearest third-party local base, with repeated double-digit showings in the ward and nearby, but would need a substantial break to win. Conservatives and Liberal Democrats have weaker ward history; the Conservative candidate’s prior Exeter result is respectable, but not enough to make him more than a minor upset threat.

Bowthorpe has been a Labour ward in the recent annual cycle, but the 2025 by-election was effectively a dead heat between Labour and Reform, which is the strongest single signal in the packet. That makes this a highly competitive three-way race rather than a safe Labour hold. I still give Labour a small edge because of the ward’s longer-term Labour pattern and the fact that Reform’s breakthrough is still relatively recent, but Reform is very close behind and could easily flip the seat. The Green candidate is the main outsider threat if the anti-Labour vote fragments or if local appeal boosts her vote, while Conservative and Liberal Democrat chances look much smaller based on recent ward history and prior results.

Peartree has been a Liberal Democrat ward throughout the recent history in the packet: the Lib Dems won in 2021, 2022, 2023, 2024, and again in the 2025 by-election. Duncan Merlin Jones also has a prior ward win and a substantial local profile, which makes him the clear favorite. The main reason not to make him close to certain is the 2025 by-election, where Reform finished a strong second and the Lib Dem margin narrowed sharply, showing the ward can become competitive under lower-turnout conditions. I discount that somewhat because by-elections are noisier than regular elections, and the regular-election record still points to a solid Lib Dem hold.

Labour is the most plausible challenger based on the longer regular-election history, where it was consistently second and sometimes fairly close. Reform has a real upset path because of its 2025 by-election strength, but it has less evidence of a durable base in the regular-cycle results. The Green candidate has local activity and some recurring vote share, but not enough historical strength to make a win likely. The Conservative candidate looks the longest shot given the recent downward pattern in ward results and lack of prior record.

Forecast as of 2026-05-01 (FPTP, 1 seat). The ward history shows substantial volatility between elections:
- Buckingham 2021: Conservative won with ~49.7% of the vote (Labour ~28.0%, Green ~11.5%, Lib Dem ~10.8%).
- Buckingham 2022: Conservative still won (~41.6%) with a relatively strong Liberal Democrat vote (~23.0%) and Labour close behind (~35.4%).
- Buckingham 2024: Labour won sharply (~50.8%), while Conservative fell to ~31.7%; Green and Lib Dem were both much weaker (Green ~5.2%, Lib Dem ~12.2%).

For 2026, the most important driver is that Labour has recently carried the seat (2024), while Conservative’s support in this specific ward has already proven capable of winning but also of dropping considerably by 2024. With no ward-level polling provided, the forecast uses a constrained, uncertainty-aware blend of:
1) Recent momentum (2024 Labour lead),
2) Long-run ward competitiveness (Conservative remains a realistic winner given prior wins), and
3) Expected candidate effects on the non-major parties.

Candidate-specific adjustments:
- 135370 (Labour, David Devoy): No direct Buckingham prior in the packet, but Labour won the ward in 2024 (and FPTP vote shares tend to persist with local party branding). So Labour is the modal winner, but not overwhelming because Labour’s 2024 share represented a swing against the Conservatives.
- 9372 (Conservative, Kevin Boram): He has prior Buckingham vote shares in the packet (49.7% in 2021; 31.7% in 2024). Running again keeps him credible. The forecast therefore gives a substantial (but smaller than Labour) chance, allowing for another reversion/oscillation.
- 77009 (Green, Gabrielle Crisp): The packet shows Green success in Adur at St Nicolas (46.8% and election win in 2021), suggesting Green can be more than a protest/minority list. However, Green was only ~5.2% in Buckingham in 2024, so the forecast keeps Green in a single-digit-to-low-double-digit range rather than matching their other ward performance.
- 111974 (Liberal Democrats, William Harpley): The only provided prior record in the packet (Marine 2024) is very low (3.05%). Buckingham’s historical Lib Dem support ranged from ~10.8% (2021) to ~23.0% (2022) to ~12.2% (2024). Given the candidate’s weak prior in the packet and the recent Buckingham downtrend (2024), this forecast keeps Lib Dem share modest.
- 135369 (Reform UK, David Stephen Cotton): No prior electoral record in the packet. In the absence of evidence of strong local support, Reform is given a small probability and a small vote share, mainly taking from the “other” pool rather than directly threatening the top two.

Turnout: Buckingham turnout in the packet is 42% (2021), 41% (2022), 44% (2024). With no further turnout information, the forecast uses a stable expectation around the recent mean, set to 43.0%.

Base rates from Deddington (single-seat FPTP) show the Conservative candidate/party has won every election in the packet’s ward history (2021–2025), typically at ~44–46% of the vote in years where the contest included comparable major-party candidates (e.g., 2024: 43.8%; 2022: 45.9%; 2025: 46.9%). Labour, Liberal Democrats, and Greens have never (in the packet’s ward history) collectively surpassed the Conservative vote share in the same election.

Candidate-level signals (same-ward, same-party where available):
- Conservative (24118, Eddie Fraser Reeves): in Deddington 2022 he achieved 45.9% and was elected. Given the ward’s consistent Conservative lead and the candidate’s prior success on the same ward, the most likely vote share range is the low-to-mid 40s.
- Labour (10830, Annette Murphy): Annette appears as Labour’s Deddington candidate in 2022–2024 and her vote share rose markedly across those elections (22.37% → 27.23% → 33.16%). In 2025, Labour’s vote share in the ward dropped to 14.37%, but that year’s Labour candidate was different (Helen Margaret Oldfield), suggesting candidate effects. For 2026, a partial rebound toward the high-20s/low-30s is plausible, but still likely below the Conservative’s expected share.
- Liberal Democrats (77050, James Hartley): his Deddington results show a clear decline (20.75% in 2022 → 18.71% in 2023 → 12.58% in 2024). That supports an expectation of a low-to-mid teens share in 2026.
- Green (10824, Aaron Bliss): his Deddington vote share is very steady across his appearances (10.99% in 2022; 10.43% in 2024; 11.13% in 2025 when he last ran). That implies ~around 10% again.
- Reform UK (128163, Gail Ann Collingwood-Turner): no prior electoral record is provided in the packet for the ward. With no historical anchor here, the safest forecast is a relatively small, single-digit vote share, but with meaningful uncertainty.

Putting these together, the mean vote shares are centered with Conservatives still leading (high 40s aren’t guaranteed, but low/mid-40s are favored), making Conservative victory the modal outcome. Labour has the largest realistic upset path because her prior Deddington trajectory (when running) reaches ~33%, but even then the Conservative lead in other elections suggests a still-substantial probability that Labour falls short.

Turnout: Deddington turnout in the packet oscillates (38% in 2024, 44% in 2025, with 40% and 42% in adjacent years). With no additional turnout-changing information in the packet as of 2026-05-01, a turnout around the middle of the recent range is most reasonable: ~42%.

As of 2026-05-01, the strongest in-ward signal in the packet is the 2025-05-01 Mincinglake & Whipton by-election: Reform UK (Anthony John Payne, 118901) won with 789 votes at 34.0% turnout; among the six listed 2025 candidates he received 34.83% of the vote (packet also reflects this: 34.83%). Labour (then represented by another candidate) polled 27.83%, with Greens 11.44%, Conservatives 8.34%, and Liberal Democrats 6.49%.

The 2026 packet includes five specific candidates and provides no evidence for any additional candidates beyond these five, so the 2025 ward vote is a useful baseline after removing the 2025 independent candidate’s votes (259) and redistributing the remainder across the parties we are forecasting (which mechanically increases the shares of Reform/Labour/Green/Con/LibDem relative to the raw 2025 percentages). That yields baseline shares approximately: Reform ~39.3%, Labour ~31.4%, Green ~12.5%, Conservative ~9.4%, LibDem ~7.3%.

Adjustments for candidate-specific uncertainty:
- 118901 (Reform): incumbency/continuity in-ward from the 2025 by-election is the main reason for his being the most likely winner; however, by-elections can regress, so his win probability is not overwhelming.
- 102891 (Labour & Co-operative): Labour’s party level has been competitive in the ward historically (Labour winners in 2021-2024), but this specific candidate is new to the provided ward history, so we keep Labour below Reform in win probability while still close in vote share.
- 120541 (Green): this candidate (Martin Ayres) is the same as the 2025 Green candidate and aligns well with prior in-ward Green support (about ~11–12%), so Greens are kept as a long shot for victory.
- 11130 (Conservative) and 11175 (Liberal Democrats): both are not the same individuals as the 2025 Con/LibDem candidates; the packet shows they have previously polled modest-to-low single-digit shares in ward-adjacent contests, so they are given lower victory probabilities.

Turnout: the ward history shows turnout of 31% (2022), 30% (2023), 34% (2024), and 34% (2025 by-election), with 35% in 2021. Candidate-level history is also broadly consistent with low-30s turnout. I therefore forecast turnout at ~33% for 2026 as a mid-point rather than forcing it to stay exactly at 34%.

Key signal is the Bowthorpe 2025 by-election (FPTP, single seat) where the top two were effectively tied: Labour (Richard Lawes) 689 votes vs Reform (Nick Taylor) 688 votes. This strongly implies a two-horse race between Labour and Reform for 2026 as well, with substantial fragmentation (Green 430; Conservative 281; Lib Dem 102).

For earlier regular elections, the ward historically strongly favoured Labour: 2021–2024 Labour won with large shares (about ~59–62%) when Reform was not on the ballot list in the packet’s ward history. That suggests that, relative to the 2025 by-election, Labour should regain some vote share in 2026 (i.e., the Labour vs Reform contest is likely closer to “Labour regains a bit but Reform remains strong” rather than a full reversal).

Candidate-specific indicators from the packet:
- Reform (113142, Nick Taylor): already demonstrated high local support in Bowthorpe in 2025 (31.42% / 688 votes). That makes Reform’s ~one-third-of-the-vote expectation plausible.
- Conservative (112676, Rebeka Jones): prior Bowthorpe performance in 2024 was much higher than in 2025 (17.62% / 342 votes in 2024 vs 281 votes in 2025). However, the 2025 by-election shows a meaningful squeeze when Reform is present.
- Green (138571, Amber Smith): no prior Bowthorpe record is provided in the packet for the specific candidate, but the party’s 2025 result in the ward (430 votes, ~19.6%) indicates the ward has a non-trivial Green vote that can persist.
- Lib Dem (41089, Neil Hardman): only non-Bowthorpe prior results are provided (modest single-digit shares elsewhere, 6.79% and 3.11%). With 102 votes in the last Bowthorpe contest for the Lib Dem candidate listed in the ward history, a low single-digit share remains most consistent.

Given this, the forecast assigns vote shares that keep Labour and Reform as the top two (Labour slightly ahead), Green as third, and Conservative/Lib Dem well below. Win probabilities reflect uncertainty around the close Labour–Reform dynamic (especially given the near tie in 2025) while still treating Green as unlikely to win under FPTP unless the top two soften meaningfully.

Seat type is single-member FPTP in Peartree ward (Welwyn Hatfield local election, 2026-05-07). With no polling or demographic shocks provided in the packet, the forecast is anchored to the ward’s recent vote patterns (2021–2025) and the limited candidate-specific electoral history in/near Peartree.

1) Baseline party strength from ward results
- Liberal Democrats (LD) have been the most consistently successful option in Peartree across multiple elections:
  - 2022: LD 51.78% (and elected)
  - 2023: LD led with 582 votes (44.6% share of candidate votes in that election)
  - 2024: LD led with 621 votes (43.3%)
  - The main recent anomaly is the 2025-05-01 by-election where LD fell to 420/1227 ≈ 34.2% (elected, but a reduced margin).
- Labour has typically been second around ~36% in 2023–2024 (466/1306 ≈ 35.7% and 521/1435 ≈ 36.3%), but dropped in 2025 to 243/1227 ≈ 19.8%.
- Green has been stable at low single digits of candidate votes:
  - 2024: 118/1435 ≈ 8.2%
  - 2025: 91/1227 ≈ 7.4%
- Conservatives were in the ~12–13% range in 2021–2024 but collapsed in the 2025 by-election (98/1227 ≈ 8.0%), with Reform UK taking a large share that year.
- Reform UK appears only in 2025 in the packet history for this ward (375/1227 ≈ 30.5%), implying substantial uncertainty about whether that level is repeatable or reverses to other right-of-centre parties.

2) Candidate-specific signals from the packet
- Candidate 93637 (Duncan Merlin Jones, LD): has an on-ward win in Peartree (elected in 2022 with 51.78% per the packet). That supports LD’s continued plurality chances.
- Candidate 111614 (Tanya Dickson, Green): has direct Peartree performance twice (8.22% in 2024; 7.42% in the 2025 by-election), suggesting Green’s share is likely to remain near this band.
- Candidate 18082 (Darren Lewis, Labour): no prior Peartree ward vote is provided in the packet, but there is a Labour result in the adjacent Handside & Peartree division in 2025 County Council election (13.54%). This is consistent with Labour being competitive but not necessarily close to the LD or Reform peaks observed in recent Peartree ward counts.
- Candidates 130636 (Reform UK) and 130655 (Conservatives): no prior election results are provided in the packet for Peartree. Therefore, their forecast shares are based mainly on the 2025 by-election party-level outcomes and the likelihood of partial mean reversion.

3) Mapping baseline to 2026 vote shares
Because 2025 saw an unusually strong Reform surge and a weaker LD vote share, the safest assumption without polling is partial regression toward the more typical 2023–2024 pattern:
- LD likely remains the plurality contender, but may not return fully to the ~43–45% level from 2023–2024 given the 2025 slip and typical by-election volatility.
- Reform could plausibly remain elevated versus Conservatives, but may soften from 2025’s very high share.
- Labour is modeled as second, though not necessarily back at its 2023–2024 peak due to the 2025 drop.
- Conservatives are modeled lower than their 2023–2024 band because the 2025 collapse is the most recent evidence in the packet.

Accordingly, the vote shares are set to:
- LD 37%, Labour 22%, Reform 27%, Conservatives 7%, Green 7% (sums to 100).

4) Converting vote shares to win probabilities
Under FPTP, the winner is determined by which candidate has the highest vote share. With LD as the modeled plurality (37%) but with Reform close (27% vs 37% still leaves room for uncertainty), LD is most likely to win but not overwhelmingly so. Green and Conservatives are given very low winning probabilities because their modeled shares are far from the top two and historically they have not won this ward in the packet’s recent elections.

5) Turnout
Turnout in the ward elections shown in the packet varies:
- 2022: 28%
- 2023: 24%
- 2024: 26%
- 2025 by-election: 22%
With 2026 being a regular May local election date (and no further information provided that turnout will be exceptionally low or high), a turnout estimate of 24% is used, roughly centered between the 2023–2024 levels and downweighted slightly due to the most recent 2025 low turnout.

Forecasting the single-seat Buckingham ward (FPTP) result as of 2026-05-01, using only the supplied packet.

1) Best local signal: prior Buckingham ward vote margins
- 2024-05-02 (most recent in this ward): Labour and Co-operative (Nigel Jenner) 681 votes vs Conservative (Kevin Boram) 424. That implies Labour led by a large margin in raw votes (about 50.8% vs 31.7% of the listed vote totals). This makes Labour the baseline favorite for 2026.
- 2022-05-05: Conservative (Emma Evans) 520 vs Labour (Joseph James O’Halloran) 442 (Conservative led by a narrower margin).
- 2021-05-06: Conservative (Kevin Boram) 646 vs Labour (Matt Bannister) 364 (Conservative led more clearly).
Overall, the ward shows a swing from Conservative dominance (2021/2022) to a Labour win in 2024. With no further 2026 polling data in the packet, the most defensible starting point is that Labour’s 2024 position remains a slight structural advantage, but not a lock, because Conservative previously performed strongly in this ward.

2) Candidate-specific information available in the packet
- Labour candidate (135370, David Joseph Devoy): No prior electoral record is provided in the packet, so we can’t quantify personal vote share. However, Labour’s brand and recent success in Buckingham (2024 win) provide most of the explanatory power.
- Conservative candidate (9372, Kevin Boram): Has strong personal linkage to Buckingham in the packet: he was Conservative in 2021 (elected), and appears again in 2024 (non-winning with 424 votes). His repeated candidacy suggests continuity of Conservative support, but the 2024 deficit vs Labour is still substantial.
- Green candidate (77009, Gabrielle Crisp): No prior Buckingham performance in the packet, but a strong prior Green result elsewhere in Adur (46.84% in St Nicolas in 2021). Still, Green’s Buckingham strength in recent elections appears limited (e.g., 70 votes in 2024, and 149 votes in 2021), so Green is modeled as a contender for vote share but not as the most likely winner.
- Liberal Democrats (111974, William Harpley): Only a very small prior Adur result is provided (3.05% in Marine in 2024). That suggests a low floor to compete for first place in Buckingham, though he could take some minor-party votes.
- Reform UK (135369, David Stephen Cotton): No prior electoral record is provided, so uncertainty is high; nevertheless, with no evidence in the packet of strong local traction, his win probability is kept very low.

3) Calibrated win probabilities
- Labour is given the highest chance (0.53) primarily due to the 2024 Buckingham win with a clear vote margin, but reduced below 0.6 because Conservative has repeatedly been competitive in this ward historically (2021/2022), and because minor parties (especially Green) could still affect vote transfers.
- Conservative is set as the second-most likely winner (0.41) reflecting continuity of a known Buckingham candidate and historically strong Conservative performance, tempered by the large 2024 loss to Labour.
- Greens (0.035) are assigned a small but non-trivial chance based on the candidate’s strong Green record elsewhere, while respecting the ward’s relatively modest recent Green vote totals.
- Liberal Democrats (0.015) and Reform UK (0.01) are assigned low probabilities due to limited/no supporting local vote evidence in the packet.

These probabilities sum to 1 and represent an uncertain but Labour-favored outcome based on the most recent ward-level evidence available.

Forecast as of 2026-05-01 using only the supplied packet and prior Deddington ward results (FPTP, one seat).

Key empirical anchors from 2021–2025 ward elections:
- Conservative consistently wins and typically polls in the mid-40% range when it has a strong candidate: e.g., 2022 (Con 45.9%), 2023 (Con 43.8%), 2024 (Con 43.8%), 2025 by-election (Con 46.9%).
- Labour has improved under Annette Murphy in earlier contests (22.37% in 2022 → 27.23% in 2023 → 33.16% in 2024), but even at its best prior point in this packet it is still substantially below the Conservative winner shares in the same ward (e.g., 2024: Cons 43.8% vs Labour 33.1%).
- Liberal Democrats show large volatility by year/candidate: in this packet’s same-post history they range from 20.75% (2022) down to 12.58% (2024) for the candidate running in 2026 (James Hartley), while a different Lib Dem candidate in 2025 achieved a much higher share (27.6%). This volatility keeps Lib Dem win odds non-zero, but for this specific 2026 candidate the historical baseline is lower than the 2025 spike.
- Green (Aaron Bliss) is steady but far from the leading pair: around ~10–11% in the years where the candidate appears (2022–2025), implying an outside chance only.
- Reform UK (Gail Ann Collingwood-Turner) has no prior electoral record in the packet and therefore is modeled as a low-probability longshot for outright victory; it could matter mainly by absorbing votes and affecting whether Labour/Lib Dem can close the gap, rather than being the most likely plurality winner itself.

Putting this together:
- The Conservative candidate (Eddie Reeves) is the modal plurality winner given the strong historical pattern and the fact that Labour/Lib Dem peaks (in the packet) typically do not surpass the Conservative vote levels.
- Labour’s path to victory would require a large and sustained increase relative to its best recorded share for Murphy (33.16%) and/or a meaningful reduction in Conservative share; that is possible but not the modal outcome.
- Lib Dem could win in a scenario combining a Conservative dip with a large Lib Dem rebound toward the upper bound seen in 2025, but the running candidate’s prior baseline is lower.

Hence the calibrated win probabilities: Conservative heavily favored (0.78), Labour a secondary chance (0.13), Lib Dem a smaller chance (0.06), Green an outside chance (0.02), and Reform minimal (0.01).

Key signal is the most recent election in this exact ward (Mincinglake & Whipton) on 2025-05-01 (by-election): Reform UK (Anthony John Payne, candidate 118901) won with 789 votes, with Labour close behind at 630. Green (Martin Ayres, candidate 120541) and the Conservatives followed at much lower levels (251 and 189), while Liberal Democrats were lowest among the five listed (147). 

For 2026-05-07 (FPTP, single seat), I treat that 2025 result as the strongest baseline for likely vote-splitting in this ward. 
- Candidate 118901 is the recent ward winner (incumbent/continuation effect), so they have the highest win probability.
- Labour (102891) is the main alternative historically (Labour won the ward outright in 2021–2024), but the specific Labour candidate provided in the packet has limited strength signals from other places (vote shares of 6.11% and 2.15% in prior council contests), so I expect Labour to be competitive but not as likely to reclaim the seat as Reform is to hold it.
- Conservatives (11130) have shown moderate support in other Exeter-area local elections (23.84% in Pinhoe 2024), but the ward-level history for Conservatives in Mincinglake & Whipton is much weaker in recent years (e.g., 189 in 2025), making a win less likely without a large local shift.
- Greens (120541) have a credible but non-leading track record in this ward’s recent election (251 votes in 2025) and should remain a meaningful third/fourth force; they can win only under a two-front split (e.g., Reform and Labour both underperforming).
- Liberal Democrats (11175) have consistently low vote shares in this ward historically (e.g., 5–10% in 2022–2024, and 147 votes in 2025), so their chance of finishing first is small.

Given these relative signals and the uncertainty introduced by new candidate profiles for some parties, the calibrated probabilities favor Reform holding the seat, with Labour the most likely challenger; the remaining parties are assigned smaller but non-zero probabilities due to possible vote-splitting swings.

Forecast as of 2026-05-01 for Bowthorpe (single-seat FPTP).

Key empirical anchors from the ward history in the packet:
- 2024 general ward election (4-candidate race): Labour won strongly (Sue Sands 1201 votes; ~61.9% of votes), with Conservatives second (~17.6%), Green third (~11.6%), and Liberal Democrats fourth (~8.9%).
- 2025 Bowthorpe by-election (5-candidate race with Reform present): Labour and Reform were essentially tied for first (Labour 689 vs Reform 688; Labour ~31.5%, Reform ~31.4%). Green then took a clear third (~19.6%), Conservatives ~12.8%, and Liberal Democrats fell to ~4.7%.

Candidate/party continuity in this 2026 field:
- Labour (Mike Sands, candidate_id 138569) is associated with prior success in this ward history (Mike Sands elected in 2022), and Labour remained competitive even when Reform surged in 2025. This drives the highest win probability.
- Reform (Nick Taylor, candidate_id 113142) performed exceptionally in the same ward by-election context in 2025 (~31.4%); since Reform is again on the ballot, the Labour–Reform contest remains the most plausible path to victory.
- Green (Amber Smith, candidate_id 138571) previously polled strongly in the 2025 5-way fragmentation (~19.6%) and could win only if one of the top two falls more than expected; hence a non-trivial but smaller win probability.
- Conservatives (Rebeka Jones, candidate_id 112676) range from ~12.8% (2025) to ~17.6% (2024). They are unlikely to surpass a Labour–Reform first-place share, but they have some chance if the vote splits unusually.
- Liberal Democrats (Neil Hardman, candidate_id 41089) are weak in this ward when Reform is present (~4.7% in 2025) and have low vote shares in other local contests listed in the packet, so their probability of winning the seat is very low.

Overall: because the 2026 candidate lineup closely resembles the 2025 multi-party structure where Labour and Reform nearly tied, the forecast centers on a close Labour-vs-Reform race with Green as a possible spoiler, while Conservative and Liberal Democrat win chances are comparatively small.

Using only the packet’s ward history (Peartree) and the candidates’ prior results in this ward or on the same post:

- Baseline strength (party level): Liberal Democrats have won every Peartree election shown in the packet (2021–2024, and the 2025 by-election also). Even in 2025, when the Liberal Democrats’ vote share fell to ~34% (420 of 1227 total votes), they still won plurality.
- Labour trajectory: Labour has been consistently competitive (2nd place in 2021–2024), but in the 2025 by-election Labour fell to ~20% and placed 3rd (behind Liberal Democrats and Reform). That suggests Labour’s win chances depend on avoiding that “vote-loss” pattern—plausible, but less certain.
- Green Party: Greens have been stable at low single-digit shares across 2024 and prior cycles (e.g., 8.22% in 2024 Peartree; 7.42% in the 2025 by-election). With FPTP and no sign of a step-change in this packet, winning looks unlikely.
- Reform and Conservative (new/limited local record in this packet): Reform appears strongly only in the 2025 by-election (~30.6%), while Conservative’s presence there is relatively small (~8.0%) and has been lower in other recent Peartree contests (generally ~8–12%). In 2026 both Reform and Conservative are present again, so a likely right-of-centre split reduces the probability that either individual right-leaning candidate can overtake the established front-runner.
- Candidate-specific cues from prior results in/around this ward/post: 
  - Duncan Merlin Jones (Lib Dem) has already won the Peartree ward (2022) with a very large vote share (51.78%).
  - Darren Lewis (Labour) has no prior Peartree result in this packet, but his only recorded prior contest (2025 county election on “Handside & Peartree”) was not competitive (13.54%); this lowers, but does not eliminate, Labour’s win probability given Labour’s stronger historical standing in Peartree.
  - Tanya Dickson (Green) shows prior Peartree vote shares (8.22% in 2024; 7.42% in 2025 by-election), consistent with a low probability of winning.
  - Ian Colpitts (Reform) and Emil Sohrab (Conservative) have no prior electoral record included in this packet, increasing uncertainty; however, historical party-level performance plus the likely split keeps their win probabilities modest.

Overall, the combination of (1) consistent Liberal Democrat wins in this ward, (2) Labour being more variable in the most recent by-election, and (3) Greens being structurally low in these results leads to a clear but not overwhelming favorite for the Lib Dem candidate.

The contest is primarily between Conservative candidate Kevin Boram and Labour candidate David Joseph Devoy. Boram won Buckingham in 2021 and was the Conservative runner-up in 2024, giving him the strongest demonstrated ward-level record among the current candidates. However, Labour won the ward in 2024 by a substantial margin, so Labour remains approximately equally competitive despite fielding a new candidate. The five-way contest creates additional vote fragmentation: Reform UK has no prior ward record but could draw support from the Conservative base, while the Green candidate has prior experience as an elected Adur councillor and a substantive local statement, making a stronger showing than the Greens' 2024 ward result plausible. The Liberal Democrat candidate has only a weak prior result in another Adur ward, so a win is unlikely. Expected turnout is set near the ward's recent 41%–44% range.

The Conservative candidate is the clear starting favorite: the Conservative ticket won every Deddington contest in the packet, with 43%–47% of the vote in 2022–2025, and Eddie Fraser Reeves previously won this ward. The main counterweight is Labour's steady rise from 22.4% in 2022 to 33.2% in 2024 under Annette Murphy, making Labour the most credible challenger. The 2025 contest showed that the Liberal Democrats can still be competitive, but their share under James Henry Dean Hartley declined substantially in the 2022–2024 regular contests, while the 2025 result was stronger than that trend. Aaron Bliss and the Green Party have a consistent floor around 9%–11% but no evidence of being close to winning. Reform UK has no prior electoral record in the packet; its presence creates additional uncertainty, particularly through possible vote fragmentation, but provides little direct evidence of a winning position. The vote-share estimate therefore gives the Conservatives a plurality, with meaningful Labour and Liberal Democrat chances in a fragmented five-way race. Turnout is set near the recent historical range, slightly below the 2025 level, with the ward's prior results ranging from 38% to 44%.

Reform UK is the clear but not overwhelming favorite because Anthony John Payne is the incumbent who won this ward in 2025 with 34.83% and 789 votes, ahead of Labour's 27.8%. The ward has been volatile: Labour won from 2021 through 2024, while Reform prevailed in 2025, and the 2024 result was close between Labour and an Independent. Labour therefore retains a credible route back, particularly with a stronger party vote than its candidate's prior personal record suggests. The Conservative candidate has a comparatively strong prior result elsewhere and could recover from the party's low 2025 ward share, but the recent ward evidence places the Conservatives behind the two leading parties. Green and Liberal Democrat candidates have established but smaller bases and are unlikely to win absent a major three-way vote split. Turnout is centered near the recent 34% level, with the historical range of 30% to 35% indicating moderate uncertainty.

This is primarily a Labour–Reform contest. Labour won every regular Bowthorpe election in the packet from 2021 through 2024, generally by a substantial margin, but that advantage disappeared in the 2025 by-election: Labour won with 689 votes while Reform UK’s Nick Taylor received 688. Taylor is returning and has demonstrated strong local support, making Reform highly competitive. Labour’s historical ward strength and the possibility of a stronger regular-election operation give Mike Sands a small edge overall. The Greens have a substantial third-place base, rising to 19.6% in 2025, and Amber Smith’s long local residence and community-focused profile could preserve that support, but the historical results provide little basis for a Green win. The Conservatives have fallen from 17.6% in 2024 to 12.8% in 2025, while the Liberal Democrats have generally polled in the low single digits. Turnout is estimated near the 28–29% levels recorded in 2022 and 2023, with a modest allowance for variation.

The Liberal Democrats are the clear but not certain favorite. They have won every Peartree contest in the packet, and Duncan Merlin Jones previously won the ward with 51.78% of the vote. The main uncertainty comes from the 2025 by-election, where the Liberal Democrat lead narrowed substantially and Reform UK finished close behind; this supports a meaningful Reform win probability, especially with Reform having recently taken 33.1% of valid votes. Labour has historically been the main challenger, but its 2025 Peartree share fell to 21.4%, while the new Labour candidate has only a 13.54% result in a different ward. Tanya Dickson's Green support has been consistently around 7–8%, making a Green win very unlikely. The Conservative vote is also likely to be squeezed by Reform and the two larger established parties. Turnout is estimated at 25.5%, near the 24–28% range of the regular ward elections and above the 22% recorded for the 2025 by-election.

Labour is the narrow favorite because it won Buckingham in 2024 by a substantial margin, although its current candidate is new and the 2024 result may not fully carry over. The Conservative candidate has previously won this ward and remains the main challenger, with meaningful probability of recovering from the 2024 defeat. The Green candidate has demonstrated local electoral strength elsewhere in Adur, making a stronger showing plausible, but Green support in Buckingham was modest in the most recent ward election. Reform UK could draw support, particularly from the Conservative side, but the candidate has no supplied electoral record. The Liberal Democrat candidate has very limited prior support in the packet, so is assigned only a small winning probability.

The Conservative candidate is the clear favorite. Conservatives won every contest in the ward from 2021 through 2025, with roughly 44%–47% in the four most recent elections, and Eddie Fraser Reeves previously won this ward with 45.9%. Labour is the strongest established challenger: Annette Murphy rose from 22.4% to 33.2% between 2022 and 2024, and her return creates a plausible route to regain support after Labour fell to 14.5% with a different candidate in 2025. Reform UK has meaningful upset potential because it is new to the ward and could attract enough support to divide the right-of-centre vote, but there is no prior candidate or ward result establishing it as the leading challenger. The Liberal Democrat candidate has a substantial historical base but declined from 20.8% to 12.6% in the 2022–2024 contests, while the Green candidate has been consistently near 9%–11% and has little evidence of a winning coalition.

Anthony John Payne and Reform UK are the clear forecast leaders: Payne won this ward's 2025 single-seat election with 34.83% and 789 votes, ahead of Labour on 27.79%, and is the only leading candidate returning from that contest. Labour remains the principal alternative given its wins in every ward contest from 2021 through 2024, but its 2026 candidate is different from the 2025 runner-up. The Conservative candidate has a stronger prior result elsewhere than the party achieved in this ward in 2025, but the ward history shows a substantial decline in Conservative support. The Green and Liberal Democrat candidates have comparatively weak ward records, making a win unlikely.

Labour is the slight favorite because it won every regular Bowthorpe election in the packet from 2021 through 2024, and Mike Sands is also the named Labour winner in 2022. However, that historical advantage weakened sharply in the 2025 by-election: Labour won by just one vote while Reform UK’s Nick Taylor nearly prevailed, making this a highly competitive Labour–Reform contest. Reform receives substantial probability because Taylor is returning after that near-win and had previously risen to 31.42% in a Norwich ward contest. The Green candidate has a meaningful outside chance given the party’s 2025 Bowthorpe vote and Amber Smith’s longstanding local connection and detailed community profile. The Conservatives and Liberal Democrats have weaker recent results in this ward, so their winning probabilities are very low.

The Liberal Democrat candidate is the strongest favorite. Liberal Democrats won every recorded Peartree contest from 2021 through 2025, Duncan Merlin Jones personally won the 2022 contest with 51.78%, and the party retains a clear historical base. However, the 2025 by-election was much more competitive: Reform UK finished only 45 votes behind the Liberal Democrats, making Reform the principal upset threat and assigning it a substantial winning probability. Labour has generally been the main historical challenger, but its recent Peartree result was well behind both Liberal Democrats and Reform, and its current candidate's supplied prior result was relatively modest in a neighboring ward. The Green candidate has a consistent but small vote base and the Conservative candidate has no supplied prior record in the ward, so both have very low chances of winning.
